Tibetan-Tones.title: Tibetan Tones
Tibetan-Tones.content:
I was investigating if the tone only exists on the first syllable as said in Manual of Standard Tibetan. I made monks record a sentence to check if I could appreciate this phenomena but I struggled to do it, because the sentence I gave might have been long.
Experiment
I will present the results from the experiment Gen Alexandra at SINI told me about the tones on syllables that are not the first.
I am asking Tibetans to send me voice notes saying ངའི་ནང་ལ་ཕེབས་རོགས་གནང་། and ང་ཚོས་ བོད་ལ་ གནས་མཇལ་ལ་ འགྲོ་དུས་ བླ་མ་རིན་པོ་ཆེ་ ཇལ་བྱུང་། ཡིན་ན་ཡང་ ཁོང་གིས་ མཇལ་དར་ གནང་མ་སོང།
My hypothesis is that according to Nicholas Tournadre as described in The Manual of Standard Tibetan there should not be tone in the pink one because only first syllable in word carries tones, the tone in the blue one is to be low tone and in the red one high tone.
So here are some recordings from Tibetan-speaking friends:






Conclusion
I struggle to appreciate the tone nuances, so I am not convinced about the hypothesis.
Responses
After speaking to Dimitri from the SINI Colloquial Tibetan course he said:

Hi, Ramon. You asked a question about tones. And mentioned Tournadre who says: no tones after the 1st syllable. We could chat about it. But it’s true. Only the tone of the 1st syllable is “kept” while the rest of the word becomes neutral. What it means in practice is a bit more complicated: usually, the tone of the second syllable kinda travels in the opposite direction of the first syllable’s tone. But not far. One can hear it very well if one pays attention to the pronounciation of the native speakers in our Anki cards.
[…]
You might find the following also interesting and informative: [www2.lawrence.edu/fast/sungk/]
[…]
Especially the last lesson (4) helps answer your question. But I think it’s really too much detail. The rule I derived from it: The second syllable is more neutral than the first one (in terms of its tone) and is a bit like an “afterthought”. In Anki they are very clear with the tone of the first syllable and the second is really neither high nor low. So, it kinda compensates the first syllable. If the first one is high, the second one returns back to lower. If the first one is low, the second returns back to higher.
I think for us as non-native speakers it’s a bit of an overkill, actually.

Eloi my linguist friend from Barcelona Tibet House said:

The point here is that the “neutral tone” is higher than the low but lower than the high. And in addition, Tibetan does not have a prosodic accent but the accent always stretches towards the end (like French). This creates the illusion of a mobile accent.

Ani Tenzin Wangmo, fellow student from my learning group:

More about tones and genders of sounds/letters are in Tagjug grammar book,  which is considered advanced level,  related to written Tibetan.

Tibetan-Tournadre-Phonetical-Transcripti.title: Tibetan Tournadre Phonetical Transcription
Tibetan-Tournadre-Phonetical-Transcripti.content:
tibetan
A very solid approach is laid down in the Manual of Standard Tibetan in the Appendix 7.
For people who don’t have the book I can see they collected this in the The Tibetan & Himalayan Library under THL Simplified Phonetic Transcription of Standard Tibetan
Summary
The correspondence between Tibetan letters and Wylie letters is the exact same except for ཅ་ which is cha instead of ca, and འ which we write as a instead of ‘a.
Superscribed letters (ར་ ལ་ ས་) fall down and disappear during transcription. and so do prefixes (ག་ ད་ བ་ མ་ འ་) and silent suffixes (ས་ ད་).
But some superscribed letters or prefixes (specially when not at beginning of word) sometimes they can be pronounced so we transliterate them in, such as བློ་བཟང་ “Lobsang” or རྡོ་རྗེ་ “Dorje”. So that leads to the question: 33. Transcribing Tibetan Words When does the prefix letter sound?
Vowels a, o, u followed by ད་ ན་ ལ་ ས་ acquire umlaut ä, ö, ü.


                  
                  Note
                  
                

I personally will do what many people commonly do to avoid umlaut in ä they approximate it to e, so instead of Jampäl they write Jampel or Thubten instead of Thubtan.


Suffixes ག་ བ་ get devoiced as k, p.
བ་ at second syllable of word is wa.
པྱ་ cha, ཕྱ cha, བྱ ja.
About ར་-attached clusters: tr, thr, dr.
Voiced radicals ག་ ཇ་ ད་ བ་ ཛ་ or a cluster གྱ་ བྱ་ without the prefix that causes aspiration loss is transcribed as gh, jh, dh, bha, dzh, ghy, jh.
དབྲ་ r, དབྱ་ y, དབ་ w.

What-Lama-in-Name-Means.title: What Lama in Name Means
What-Lama-in-Name-Means.content:
Today, in the hike that I did with some monks we lost one of them at some point, and I thought to let know a shopkeeper that if he sees a monk to let him know that we continued the way up after having waited 5 minutes. When the monk translated my message into Nepali he mentioned Lama and it surprised me if that was the translation of the word “monk”.
I asked if lama can be used for any Buddhist monk and he said yes. I remembered that some monks have the word Lama in their IDs, and that was part of the family lineage, or cast as they say in Nepal, and it seems it’s also that way. If your father is from a strong Buddhist lineage you would probably inherit the Lama without being actually a monk.
The thing with the casts in Nepal confuses me a little, since then it means you can be cast + Lama, e.g. Gurung Lama, and it is like this because the Hotel Manager at Pema Tsal’s name is Rigzin Gurung Lama. My friend told me also could be the case with Sherpa Lama, Limbu Lama and other combinations.

for file in spanish-*.mp3
do
  ffmpeg -i "$file" -af "volume=2.0" "modified-$file"
done

Now listen to them and if happy, replace the original files:
for file in spanish-*.mp3
do
  mv "modified-$file" "$file"
done

If you have the original recordings in one folder and created anohter folder where you want only to have the amplified recordings:
for file in ../Original\ Volume/*.mp3; do ffmpeg -i "$file" -af "volume=2.0" $(basename $file); done

Drafts/Asians-Don't-Show-Publicly.title: Asians Don't Show Publicly
Drafts/Asians-Don't-Show-Publicly.content:
Mostly I’m writing about my experience in Nepal and India.
Showing Intimacy
Couples at street would never kiss or place their hands on the partner’s but cheeks for instance.
However, same-gender friends can put arm around each other or even hold hands. In Solukhumbu I saw even the military soldiers holding hands.
I also saw how monks in the monastery are also very “touchy” when the talk with you, they grab your hand and stroke it, or hold it as walking and talking. It’s a normal thing and doesn’t have any connotation. I couldn’t see any follow up or suggestive sign that would carry the touching to something more intimate!
Clothing
Male nor females don’t show their legs, even if it’s very warm. They just get thinner pants but I hardly have seen Nepalis wearing shorts on the street.
Public Showers
As explained in 2025-03-16, after swimming we showered. Each shower had separators to block the view as you shower, so I was completely naked. After coming out of the shower with my towel on, the kids looked stranged saying “Why don’t I wear something while I shower”. I told them in gyms or any other public spaces where people shower nobody does that in Spain. So I learned Nepalis really don’t like exposing parts of the bodies so they can be accidentally watched by others. Same with short pants, they don’t like them, even when it’s very warm!

Drafts/Begna's-Lake-Shipwreck.title: Begna's Lake Shipwreck
Drafts/Begna's-Lake-Shipwreck.content:
On March 21th 2025 I took a boat around 5pm to sail within Begna’s Lake. It was a bit windy but not too much. I went to the shore where the Begna’s Lake Resort is and I had a chat with the hotel boy that came running down towards me to see who I was and what intentions I had, he was very friendly.
After that I went back into my boat and starting rowing back to my origin on the other shore: Lake Restaurant & Cottage. But the wind started intensifying and there was no way I could get there, I was being pushed inwards. You can see it in iCloud Photos 2025-03-21 18:03.
I called the guesthouse manager and he said he would try to find help. I felt quite scared because the boat was also doing a tumbling motion that I don’t know if would throw me into the water or not. If this happened I pictured how hard would it be to swim back to shore with these waves, and there’s no way I wanted to be in this situation. Also the sun was already set. Also my iPhone would die and I rely too much on it for 2-factor authentications and important documents and payments. I was getting very tired… My arms were starting to fail in the rowing.
I rowed as hard as I could but I would not move, and if I did only a tiny bit. The wind would change my direction all the time so I would not point towards shore, but I aimed again and insisted with my muscles. At some point I saw a golf or bay—I don’t know which word to use—which was not affected by the wind and I aimed there but the wind was too strong and ended up landing me into another kind of golf / bay, following the instructions from another guy that called me (it was the guest house manager’s uncle).
I tried to row inland but there were many floating sea weeds that would prevent me to do so, so at the end I tied it into a tree root and started climbing the mountain. It was dark. I light on the torch and started climbing. My flip-flops were wet and slippery, so I removed them, I didn’t care about getting scratches, but I was scared of snakes. Usually snakes arrive with rainy season, and many are deadly if they bite you. The jungle was dense, and the slope too much, so it took me a good while and few falls to go inwards, following Google Maps GPS.
The person that was tracking me was asking to send WhatsApp location, but WhatsApp was not working, only phone call. So I started announcing my position by shouting. He said a rescue boat with 3 fishermen was scanning the shore in search for me.
At some point I met the fisherman and I jumped into their boat, and that ride was magical.
Now the water was calm, and the skies were strange: electrical storm with no sound was taking place. The sky was flashing at distance, but no thunders. There were some fires at distance too, I don’t know if it was produced by the lightning or simply the farmer burning bad weeds. Then I realised about those yellow flashing points of light on the shore… They were fireflies! What a surreal moment! It reminds me of the moment I keep explaining to friends of when I was in the Icelandic farm, with the fireplace in the black sand beach, with the blue of the waves, the pink sky, blue mountain snow tops, green grass fields… So magical…
The fishermen shared a rolled cigarette or a weed joint, I don’t know what was it. They were very friendly. When we arrived at the guesthouse I broke my vow not to smoke and asked for a cigarette.
Then I got worried about a wound in my feet that looked a bit like an animal bite. If it was a snake I would be quite done, because my area is quite remote and far from hospitals. So I messaged Elisa Sanz just to see if the condition was critical. It didn’t look like it however she was not too sure. In the end it probably was a scratch, since otherwise I would not be writing this note.

Drafts/Irene's-Blissful-Spin.title: Irene's Blissful Spin
Drafts/Irene's-Blissful-Spin.content:
In Pema Tsal I met with Irene, a beautiful soul. She enjoyed listening to my esoteric experiences and I enjoyed listening to hers.
She is a doctor in social sciences, always wanted to be in control of everything, so lead an intense life with the anxieties and mental imbalances and excessive will to control brings you. At some point she developed a cancer and followed chemo. She was getting very weak, and always wanted to still be in control and grasp of herself.
But one day during treatment, and after having done some Reiki-like practices her body was weakening and a sense of dizziness was arising. She didn’t want to keep forcing holding the posture anymore and surrendered to it. At that moment a sensation of endless comfortable spinning was happening simultaneously whilst she was still aware of the environment. That was very pleasant, and felt like a blissful revelation. When the practitioner came she didn’t want to speak yet in case it would stop the feeling.
She thought this feeling was due to chemo, but later on experienced it 3 times more in her life, and that brought so much joy to her. She wants to investigate what it is but doesn’t know where to go and asked me if I ever see something similar happening to someone to message her.
She sees it as a kind of energetic connection with the universe.
She emphasizes it’s a simultaneous thing and she can function normally as it happens, so when I compared it to Vipassana’s stage bhanga (dissolution) she said it’s not so much like it.

Drafts/Publish-Obsidian-Notes.title: Publish Obsidian Notes
Drafts/Publish-Obsidian-Notes.content:
Goals

Ability to select what gets published and what not..
Media attachments to get included, and also audio.
Tibetan text to be read properly.

❌ Option 1: Obsidian Export
github.com/zoni/obsidian-export
How it Works: A subfolder can be exported with a tool such as obsidian-export , which also brings with it all the embedded files into my personal’s website content folder which can render .mdx.
I was doing my own customization to handle .mdx and embed the images and audios as I wanted in eulersson/obsidian-mdx-export.  I ended up deleting because it would involve a lot of work on designing the components on the personal website side. I would be reinventing the wheel maybe the benefit of some seamlessness with my website but I don’t think it’s worth.
❌ Option 2: Digital Garden
dg-docs.ole.dev/
How it Works: What is convenient about this method is having the action within Obsidian to publish the note, which by using the GitHub API would send the content to your repository without needing any local machine to do it, it goes through HTTP. Then Vercel would pick up the changes and produce the static website.
It’s the first one I tried, it produced what I intended, but compared to Option 3 Quartz it felt very basic, quartz gives you more fine-grained detail and deeper customization into the processing steps that take during the content transformation.
I had to write some changes on their code in order to also have it embed audio files. See the Pull Requests I offered to oleeskild/digitalgarden and oleeskild/obsidian-digital-garden.
✅ Option 3: Quartz
quartz.jzhao.xyz/
How it Works: You clone your quartz fork and dump the markdown files within the content/ folder. Then some hook (GitHub, Cloudflare, …) would detect the commit and run the static page generation. That means the markdowns ideally should be in the repository but I am using it in a different way because I don’t want my entire vault to be in the repository.


                  
                  Success
                  
                

Have a look! notes.ramn.dev/


My Tweaks: I symlink my vault to content/ and run the static generation locally, commit it and push it. The hook therefore (in my case Cloudflare) doesn’t need any node environment therefore, it only grabs the pages from public/ and uses that for serving. The ugly part is that generated content is kept in the repo instead of the source that generated it. One possible solution would be to write my own plugin that copies the pages to publish to that repo in the same fashion of Option 1 Digital Garden, so that I keep my vault separate.
Perks: You get the graph of interconnected notes! Also everything is a React component, so you have a lot of freedom to customize the look and feel. Furthermore you can hook into the text transformation very easily and write your own transformers. I wrote my on Tibetan language processors which wraps it into <span> HTML tags that increase the text size!
The deployment method I used is Cloudflare Pages which seems convenient since I have my domain proxied through them anyway. The instructions are in Quartz Documentation’s Hosting on Cloudflare Pages.
Check Deploying Digital Garden.
Next Steps
Having an action without Obsidian to send the content to the GitHub repository and push it so I don’t have to deal with the local commands in Deploying Digital Garden,

Drafts/Story-of-Bouddhanath-Stupa.title: Story of Bouddhanath Stupa
Drafts/Story-of-Bouddhanath-Stupa.content:
Todo
                  
                

Ask Sara in Kopan for the beautiful complete story.


The stupa was built by a poor mother who was a chicken farmer. She really wanted to build it so asked the king of Nepal permission. Usually the king would deny but “Okay, it can be done.” slipped out from its mouth, hence why the name མཆོད་རྟེན་བྱ་རུང་ཁ་ཤོར་, བྱ་རུབ་ = “it’s fine for you to make”, ཁ་ཤོར་ = “slipped from mouth”.
The woman asked to build it on the size of a buffalo skin. But the trick is that the buffalo skin was turned into a long thread that drew a circle the size of the stupa.
The mother died before completing the stupa. She had 4 sons and they finished it. That stupa is a wish-granting stupa, there are many cases. The sons themselves wished:

“May I become a Dharma king in Tibet.”
“May I become a minister to help him (the King) spread the Dharma.”
“May I be an abbot to pass on the lineage of ordinations in Tibet.”
“May I become a powerful yogi when there are obstacles to spreading Dharma in Tibet.”

They respectively became: Songtsen Gampo, Padma Guntsen, Shantarakshita, Padmasambhava.
Sources

fpmt.org/mandala/archives/mandala-issues-for-2010/april/story-of-the-bouddhanath-stupa/
Oertle. Heart of Tibetan Language, Vol II

Journal/Daily/2025-03-03.content:
<< 2025-03-02 | 2025-03-04 >>
I went for a hike to a very nice place called Sarangkot with three monks and we had a lot of fun, it’s nice to be close friends with them. Their names are ཀུན་དགའ་འཕྲིན་ལས། (Kunga Thrinle), ཀུན་དགའ་ཤེས་རབ། (Kunga Sherab) and རིན་ཆེན་དོན་གྲུབ། (Rinchen Dhöndrup).
I can see how they struggle with words that are not in the scriptures, and how my anki flashcards sometimes don’t make sense to them: for instance “bus” is སྤྱི་སྤྱོད་རླངས་འཁོར་ according to Nicolas Tournadre and Franziska Oertle, but they said they would use “bus”. I added it to 43. How do you say “bus” in Tibetan?.
I spoke Tibetan a little more loose with them and I also tried to teach some Spanish, and I saw their struggles, so I believe my slow process of learning Tibetan is OK.

With the students we went on an excursion. We ate picnic and played cards in their retreat center, then we headed to Sarangkot, a wonderful viewpoint with a temple, and finally ended up in Lakeside, where we ate pizza, and roller skated and rode bumper cars.
It has been so much fun.
In the bus—as it’s common with all buses in Nepal—there was a powerful sound system and Nepali music blasted during the bumpy ride. The kids started dancing to and singing to the songs full power, that made me very happy.
I have recorded some of that and also collected some of the songs in Music Monks Like. The one that will stay in my mind for long is Dai Nabhane Hai By Pratima Gaihre, Prakash Parajuli, Arjun Sapkota and Narayani I even was singing and dancing to it.

Today Ngawang Tashi finally returned from the Losar break. We were so happy to see each other and he gave me a heavy bag with ཁ་ཟས་ (Tibetan biscuits) from his village and 100 rupees I could not refuse. I still can’t believe I cannot make kids understand I don’t want them to give me rupees.
Finally finished watching all the videos from Franziska.
I felt a bit derealised while playing games with the children in the afternoon and also when having lunch with Lobsang Tsering. I think I should take it easy and do less work and more quality… I am digesting many Tibetan language things and I don’t believe those things are sticking as well as I would like, but sometimes I am impressed the memory finds the right word when interacting in Tibetan with monks around.
Revised the written notes that I take whilst following Tibetan courses and produced an index out of it.
Anki as always. Today it has been faster to finish.

<< 2025-03-13 | 2025-03-15 >>
Today I sponsored the lunch from the class 8 students.
One of the monks was from China! He told me his story and I felt amazed. His parents are from Beijing, and when he was little he wanted to be a monk, he felt wonder for monastic life. So parents brought him to Tibet and then stayed there as a monk student. Later on the parents were advised by Sakya Trizin to send him to Pema Tsal.
I thought someone from Beijing would never send the child to Tibetan monasteries outside of China, to avoid problems. It’s very interesting.

Journal/Daily/2025-03-16.content:
<< 2025-03-15 | 2025-03-17 >>
Another fun day out with the monks.
We hiked to Gharmi View Point and we threw paper airplanes from there. Afterwards we played some burrito games while we waited for our momos and chowmein (which Tashi Dhöndrup sponsored) and then we skated in Bhat Batheni, a shopping mall.
To get to the skate park we took the elevator—which by the way had glass view. They never had been to one so it was so much fun to see the awe on their faces.
On the way down they discovered the escalators and they literally wanted to go up and down 5 times; they hadn’t used one before either.
Then we went to ride bycicle in Lakeside. I taught Ngawang Tashi a little bit more, he is getting more skilful, but Tashi Dhöndrup seems to have picked it up quicker than I expected. Then we decided to go swimming to a public swimming pool close to Pokhara.
The sun hid behind the clouds and it was quite cold but we played for some time and raced swimming. Then when we showered I saw again Asians Don’t Show Publicly.
Also we had one of the best Pani Puri I tried in my entire life.
After that we did kayaking again with the addition of using water balloons to attack each other.
Then we headed back to Pema Tsal and I arranged the chef to prepare the food in advance so when we arrive we eat fast and go to the gates. Funnily enough they also hadn’t had Takhali before! And it’s one of their national foods!
In the shower I met a lovely guy whose brother works at a restaurant in Barcelona which I have been to! It’s called Gandi.

One of the guests at Pema Tsal inspired me with his Near Death Experience: Irene’s Blissful Spin.
I wrote the farewell letters: Tibetan Pema Tsal Farewell, Tibetan Tashi Döndrup Farewell, Tibetan Ngawang Tashi Farewell, Tibetan Bailarín Farewell.
One nun from the Tibetan course shared great insider information about Dharamsala: Dharamsala Expenses.
Today two monks told me they want to leave monk life. And explained the monastery always tries to keep the certificates and Identity Cards so they don’t leave. Even if changing monastery within the same tradition (Sakya) e.g. Namgyal Pokhara. Tseering Sherpa run away and had to make the ID card again. Not even if the family pressures, they said… I don’t know whether to believe it…
I had the first meeting with my Tibetan language course’s SLC (Small Learning Community), which is a nice way to learn in a peer-to-peer way instead of purely from teacher-to-pupil. The group is composed of me, Ani Tenzin Wangmo, a Slovenian nun, and Cécile Baudelaire, a descendant of the poet Charles Baudelaire!

I dreamt you and me went together to the bat cave. But you didn’t come and when you came you arrived and you were drunk. And then you said “I feel sorry I am drunk” and I said “Don’t worry, don’t be sad, if you cry I will cry, so let’s collect our emotions.”.

Journal/Daily/2025-03-19.content:
<< 2025-03-18 | 2025-03-20 >>
Today I left Pema Tsal.
Most of the kids were sad. We farewelled with hugs. I received many letters from them in English and Tibetan language… I was not expecting them to do that! I also gave some letters to Ngawang Tashi, Tashi Dhöndrup and Bailarín, but have not received any from them—it’s OK.
I gave a speech in Tibetan in front of students during assembly. Later on a monk told me the Headmaster scolded the students saying that “Ramon speaks Tibetan better than some of you!” 😂.
I have always strong respect and I feel a bit scared when I see the khempo. But  wanted to offer him a khatak. When I did and I said in Tibetan that “I give it now because perhaps afterwards I will be gone and won’t see him” he smiled and grabbed my nose with his thumb and index finger playfully. It’s really mysterious how joyful those lamas are yet looking stern and intimidating.

Sources/Class/Tibetan-2025-02-10.content:
The teacher writes on the board.
རྒན་ལགས་འཀར་པང་གི་སྒང་ལ་འབྲི་གི་ཡོད་རེད།


ཏག་ཏག་ to say “middle” (not big, not small) or “correct”.

ག་ས་ག་ everywhere:

From everywhere.
ག་ས་ག་ནས་


གཅིག་པུ་ another way to say “only” མ་གཏོགས་:

She only has one teacher
མོ་རང་ལ་རྒན་ལགས་གཅིག་ གཅིག་པུ་ཡོད་རེད་ / མ་གཏོགས་མེད།


To say “few”, “some” or “a little”:

For people: ཁ་ཤས་ / འགའ་ཤས་
For things: ཏོག་ཙམ་


For “lazy”:

Writing: ལེ་ལོ་
Speaking: ཉོབ་ཏོ་


ཟབ་ཟབ་ another way to say ཏོག་ཙམ་ “a little”:

Please, wait a moment.
ཏོག་ཙམ་ / ཟབ་ཟབ་ སྒུག་རོགས་གནང་།



If you grab one dog, the other gets jealous.
ཁྱེད་རང་ ཁྱི་གཅིག་ བཀྱག་པ་ ཡིན་ན་ གཞན་པ་ དེ་ ཕྲག་དོག་ཟ་གི་རེད།

to lift up:

འགྱོག་པ་ (present)
བཀྱགས་པ་ (past)
བཀྱག་པ་ (future)


བྱུང་ན་ “If possible”:

If possible, come here.
བྱུང་ན་ཁྱེད་རང་འདིར་ཤོག་དང་།


If possible, make me a tea.
བྱུང་ན་ང་ལ་ཇ་བཟོ་རོགས་གནང་།


Asking prices:

གོང་ price
གོང་ཆེན་པོ་ expensive
གོང་ཁེ་པོ་ / ཁེ་པོ་ cheap


How much does it cost?
དེ་ལ་ག་ཚོད་རེད།


This is expensive! Reduce the price!
གོང་ཆུང་ཆུང་བྱེད་རོགས་གནང་།

དེ་ལ་ག་ཚོད་རེད། གོང་ཆུང་ཆུང་བྱེད་རོགས་གནང་།
How much does it cost? Reduce the price!

འདོད་པ་ “Do you want …”

Do you want to read a book?
ཁྱེད་རང་དེབ་གློག་འདོད་ཡོད་པས།


Confusion:

རོགས་གནང་ imperative polite
དགོས་རེད་ should


I don’t want this.
འདི་ང་ལ་དགོས་ཀྱི་མི་འདུག

Sources/Books/The-Hand-Book-for-Life's-J.title: The Hand Book for Life's Journey
Sources/Books/The-Hand-Book-for-Life's-J.content:
by Khenpo Tsultrim Lodrö Rinpoche

Only an enlightened being can judge whether a person is enlightened or not.

That way you protect the criticizing from non-believers thinking they are smarter than they actually are.
Khen Rinpoche is Khenpo in Serthar Larung, a good institute that produce great practitioners, not only intellectual scholars.
The Principle of Attaining Liberation
Do not separate the three wisdoms
Do not separate hearing, contemplation and meditation or the Dharma.
If having little time rather than meditating it’s better to first hear the teachings that suit your situation the best at that moment.
What is Dharma Practice
Prostration, prayer, etc are not the most important. Practice of mind comes first. To do that secluding some time a day can help set the mind in the right state to practice.
The purpose of practicing Dharma
People lower in society get physical pain; people higher in society get mental pain. That destroys sentient brings.
The root of samsara
Attachment is the root. Self-grasping and grasping at self-nature of phenomena. This us not due to God’s will or coincidente.
The arhat does not have preference to have half body being bathed while the other being cut. They are free from desire and aversion.
Moths drawn into fire: if they knew they would die they wouls be more careful with that attachment.
Ignorance is the root of samsara. Ignorance gives rise to desire.
The principle of liberation
Finding the right remedy

How do we engage in conflict with the root of samsara or that of suffering? To start the conflict, generate renunciation; to escalate the conflict, arouse bodhicitta. Once we attain liberation of emptiness, the conflict will gradually reach its climax and come to perfect end.

Letting go is different from giving up. When you are fed up you give up, but later will be tempted to retake to the old self. Letting go is not having no selfish desire for anything.
The way to be free
First way is checking if that object really exists or if it’s an imputation of the mind. That happiness doens’t exist as we think, and that everything is impermanent.
Another way is to reflect inwardly, and see what has that attachment. Body can’t be, then is mind or conciousness? To see if conciousness can really exist Madhyamaka (its ultimate view) and Chittamatra (comventional view) practices can be combined.
Tantric Mahamudra or Dzogchen do not observe the external phenomena but go deep into the self to see the engine of greed and delusion. Through these methods clear light awareness, never tainted, is manifested at the end. Then you see desire comes from nowhere, it’s an illusion.
Without renounciation nor liberation the Dharma practice is bounded only to human real for positive reward, samsara continues.

Sources/Prayers/100-Syllables-Vajrasattv.content:
ཨོཾ་བཛྲ་སཏྭ་ས་མ་ཡ༔ མ་ནུ་པཱ་ལ་ཡ༔ བཛྲ་སཏྭ་ཏྭེ་ནོ་པ་ཏིཥྛ་དྲྀ་ཌྷོ་མེ་བྷ་ཝ༔ སུ་ཏོ་ཥྱོ་མེ་བྷ་ཝ༔ སུ་པོ་ཥྱོ་མེ་བྷ་ཝ༔ ཨ་ནུ་རཀྟོ་མེ་བྷ་ཝ༔ སརྦ་སིདྡྷི་མྨེ་པྲ་ཡཙྪ༔ སརྦ་ཀརྨ་སུ་ཙ་མེ༔ ཙིཏྟཾ་ཤྲེ་ཡཾཿ ཀུ་རུ་ཧཱུྂ༔ ཧ་ཧ་ཧ་ཧ་ཧོཿ བྷ་ག་ཝཱན༔ སརྦ་ཏ་ཐཱ་ག་ཏ་བཛྲ་མཱ་མེ་མུཉྩ་བཛྲཱི་བྷ་ཝ་མ་ཧཱ་ས་མ་ཡ་སཏྭ་ཨཱཿ

Sources/Prayers/Dedication-Prayer.title: Dedication Prayer
Sources/Prayers/Dedication-Prayer.content:
བསྡོ་བའི་སྨོན་ལམ་དང་། དྲིལ་བུ་ཡི་སྒྲ་གསུམ།
བསོད་ནམས་འདི་ཝིས་ཐམས་ཅད་གཟིགས་པ་ཡིད།།
ཐོབ་ནས་ཉིས་པའི་དགྲ་རྣམས་ཕམ་བྱས་ཤིང་།།
སྐྱེ་རྒ་ན་འཆིའི་རྦ་རླབས་འཁྲུགས་པ་ཡི།།
སྲིད་པའི་མཚོ་ལས་འགྲོ་བ་སྒྲོལ་བར་ཤོག།

Sources/Prayers/Food-Offering.title: Food Offering
Sources/Prayers/Food-Offering.content:
སྟོན་པ་བླ་མེད་སངས་རྒྱས་རིན་པོ་ཆེ། །
སྐྱོབ་པ་བླ་མེད་དམ་ཆོས་རིན་པོ་ཆེ། །
འདྲེན་པ་བླ་མེད་དགེ་འདུན་རིན་པོ་ཆེ། །
སྐྱབས་གནས་དཀོན་མཆོག་གསུམ་ལ་ཕྱག་འཚལ་ལོ། །
tönpa lamé sangye rinpoche
kyobpa lamé damchö rinpoche
drenpa lamé gendün rinpoche
kyabné könchok sum la chaktsal lo
To the precious Buddha, the unsurpassable teacher,
To the precious Dharma, the unsurpassable protector,
To the precious Sangha, the unsurpassable guide—
To our refuge, the Three Jewels, I pay homage.

Sources/Prayers/Gang-Gi-Lodröma-Jam-pel-.title: Gang Gi Lodröma Jam-pel-yang Manjushri Prayer (Pema Tsal)
Sources/Prayers/Gang-Gi-Lodröma-Jam-pel-.content:
འཇམ་དཔལ་དབྱངས་ལ་བསྟོད་པ་གང་བློ་མ། (གང་གི་བློ་གྲོས་མ།)
བླ་མ་དང་མགོན་པོ་འཇམ་པའི་དབྱངས་ལ་འཇམ་དབྱངས་ཕྱག་འཚལ་ལོ།


                  
                  Note
                  
                

(Pema Tsal) adds these here:
གཞོན་ནུ་སྐུ་ལས་འཚང་བ་པོ།
ཨོ་ཤེས་སྒྲོན་མེ་ནབ་མཇས་ཤིང་།
འཇེག་རྟེན་གསུམ་རྒྱལ་མུན་སེལ་བའི།
འཇམ་པའི་དབྱངས་ལ་ཕྱག་འཚལ་ལོ།


གང་གི་བློ་གྲོས་སྒྲིབ་གཉིས་སྤྲིན་བྲལ་ཉི་ལྟར་རྣམ་དག་རབ་གསལ་བ།
ཇི་སྙེད་དོན་ཀུན་ཇི་བཞིན་གཟིགས་ཕྱིར་ཉིད་ཀྱི་ཐུགས་ཀར་གླེགས་བམ་འཛིན།
གང་དག་སྲིད་པའི་བཙོན་རར་མ་རིག་མུན་འཐུམས་སྡུག་བསྔལ་གྱིས་གཟིར་བའི།
འགྲོ་ཚོགས་ཀུན་ལ་བུ་གཅིག་ལྟར་བརྩེ་ཡན་ལག་དྲུག་ཅུའི་དབྱངས་ལྡན་གསུང་།
འབྲུག་ལྟར་ཆེར་སྒྲོག་ཉོན་མོངས་གཉིས་སློང་ལས་ཀྱི་ལྕགས་སྒྲོག་འགྲོལ་མཛད་ཅིང་།
མ་རིག་མུན་སེལ་སྡུག་བསྔལ་མྱུ་གུ་ཇི་སྙེད་གཅོད་མཛད་རལ་གྲི་བསྣམས།
གདོད་ནས་དག་ཅིང་ས་བཅུའི་མཐར་སོན་ཡོན་ཏན་ལུས་རྫོགས་རྒྱལ་སྲས་ཐུ་བོའི་སྐུ།
བཅུ་ཕྲག་བཅུ་དང་བཅུ་གཉིས་རྒྱན་སྤྲས་བདག་བློའི་མུན་སེལ་འཇམ་པའི་དབྱངས་ལ་འདུད།
ཨོཾ་ཨ་ར་པ་ཙ་ན་དྷི།
བརྩེ་ལྡན་ཁྱོད་ཀྱི་མཁྱེན་རབ་འོད་ཟེར་གྱིས།
བདག་བློའི་གཏི་མུག་མུན་པ་རབ་བསལ་ནས།
བཀའ་དང་བསྟན་བཅོས་གཞུང་ལུགས་རྟོགས་པ་ཡི།
བློ་གྲོས་སྤོབས་པའི་སྣང་བ་སྩལ་དུ་གསོལ།

Sources/Prayers/Nepali-Anthem-Sayaun-Thu.title: Nepali Anthem Sayaun Thunga Phulka
Sources/Prayers/Nepali-Anthem-Sayaun-Thu.content:
सयौं थुँगा फूलका
सयौं थुँगा फूलका हामी, एउटै माला नेपाली
Sayauñ thuñgā phulkā hāmi, euṭai mālā Nepāli,
सार्वभौम भई फैलिएका, मेची-महाकाली ।
Sārvabhaum bhai phailiekā, Meci-Mahākāli.
प्रकृतिका कोटि-कोटि सम्पदाको आँचल
Prakritikā koṭi-koṭi sampadāko āñcala,
वीरहरूका रगतले स्वतन्त्र र अटल।
Virharukā ragatale svatantra ra aṭala.
ज्ञानभूमि, शान्तिभूमि, तराई, पहाड, हिमाल
Gyānabhumi, shāntibhumi, tarāi, pahāḍ, himāla,
अखण्ड यो प्यारो हाम्रो मातृभूमि नेपाल।
Akhaṇḍa yo pyāro hāmro mātribhumi Nepāla.
बहुल जाति, भाषा, धर्म, संस्कृति छन् विशाल
Bahul jāti, bhāṣā, dharma, sañskriti chan vishāla,
अग्रगामी राष्ट्र हाम्रो, जय जय नेपाल ।
Agragāmi rāṣṭra hāmro, jaya jaya Nepāla.

Sources/Prayers/Pema-Tsal-Morning.content:

Sources/Prayers/Prayer-to-Padmasambhava.title: Prayer to Padmasambhava
Sources/Prayers/Prayer-to-Padmasambhava.content:
༄༅། །གསོལ་འདེབས་རྒྱ་གར་པཎ་ཆེན་མ་བཞུགས།
རྒྱ་གར་པཎ་ཆེན་བོད་ལ་བཀའ་དྲིན་ཆེ། །
པདྨ་ལས་འཁྲུངས་སྐུ་ལ་འདས་གྲོངས་མེད། །
ད་ལྟ་ལྷོ་ནུབ་སྲིན་པོའི་ཁ་གནོན་མཛད། །
ཨོ་རྒྱན་རིན་པོ་ཆེ་ལ་གསོལ་བ་འདེབས། །
gyagar panchen böla kadrin ché
pema lé trung ku la dé drongmé
darta lhonup sinpö kha nön dzé
orgyen rinpoche la solwa deb

Sources/Prayers/Taking-Refuge-(Kyabdro).title: Taking Refuge (Kyabdro)
Sources/Prayers/Taking-Refuge-(Kyabdro).content:
སྐྱབས་འགྲོ
སངས་རྒྱས་ཆོས་དང་ཚོགས་ཀྱི་མཆོག་རྣམས་ལ། །
བྱང་ཆུབ་བར་དུ་བདག་ནི་སྐྱབས་སུ་མཆི། །
བདག་གི་སྦྱིན་སོགས་བགྱིས་པའི་བསོད་ནམས་ཀྱིས། །

ཐུབ་བསྟན་བསམ་འཕེལ་ནོར་བུའི་འོད་སྣང་འབར།
བསྟན་འགྲོའི་ནོར་འཛིན་རྒྱ་ཆེར་སྐྱོང་བའི་མགོན།
འཕྲིན་ལས་ཀྱི་རོལ་མཚོ་རྒྱས།

གནམ་བསྐོས་དགའ་བ་བརྒྱ་ལྡན་དབུ་འཕང་དགུང་ལ་རེག
ཕུན་ཚོགས་སྡེ་བཞིའི་མངའ་ཐང་རྒྱས།
བོད་ལྗོངས་ཆོལ་ཁ་གསུམ་གྱི་ཁྱོན་ལ་བདེ་སྐྱིད་རྫོགས་ལྡན་གསར་པས་ཁྱབ།

སྐྱེ་རྒུ་ཞི་བདེའི་དཔལ་ལ་སྦྱོར།
བོད་ལྗོངས་བསྟན་འགྲོའི་དགེ་མཚན་ཉི་འོད་ཀྱིས།
བཀྲ་ཤིས་འོད་སྣང་འབུམ་དུ་འཕྲོ་བའི་གཟིས།

Sources/Videos/Bryan-Jenks'-PKM-Workflow.title: Bryan Jenks' PKM Workflow
Sources/Videos/Bryan-Jenks'-PKM-Workflow.content:
Source: www.youtube.com/watch
Files: iCloud Other > Brian Jenks Obsidian Vault
Interesting things:

Nested tags to reflect type and status.

#book/evergreen


Kanban View.
Data view.
Shortcut to send notes from iOS.
Templates for ingestion material.
regex: content:/\w[zZ]\./
Supercharged links
I put a date and title once a note is completed
Timeline note

Utility note
Shows the ones that are not completed


Breadcrumb links between temporal components.
Horizontal panel opening

Uses tags for contextless groupings like “book”, “podcast”, “task”, or status (using emojis in his case) “incubator”, “evergreen”, etc… Instead of using topic tags “psychology”, “society”, links to non-existing pages are created for this, so it can be viewed in the graph view and then create a MOC.
No folders to reduce the cognitive load and friction of “oh, in which folder do I put this”. Using links is a more organic approach. Sometimes one thing would belong to various folders, therefore you wouldn’t copy it to all folders, instead use links which allow multiple linking.
Temporal Components
Link diary notes so you can jump from one to the other. Similarly weekly notes link to next and previous week.
They are all under Timeline note, so weekly or monthly they can be reviewed.
Weekly, the highlights of daily notes are referenced in the weekly note, and similarly with monthly notes.
Calendar plugin.
Literature note processing
Each type of input is processed differently.

Sources/Videos/Franziska-Oertle's-Learni.title: Franziska Oertle's Learning How to Learn
Sources/Videos/Franziska-Oertle's-Learni.content:
Tags: Learning
The sixth-fold taxonomy of significant learning by Dr. Fink


As soon as there is learning, all these dimensions are activated.

Foundational: Basically the ability to retain into memory theoretical or the most basic building blocks.
Application: Using those principles to compose higher structures, identify and know when to use them.
Integration: Who we are now is the result of many previous processes. The learning needs to become a part of our lives, so it shows who we are and why we are.
Caring: How emotions are involved during the learning process. The relationship between ourselves and the subject matter. To observe, be aware i embrace them as part of the process. A negative emotion can hijack the entire learning process and bail out. Transmute the negative emotions into positive through a valid motivation.
Human: Our learning is going to change the way we view ourselves and the way others view us. If the subject matter has not produced any change in how we view ourselves and how we relate with the world around us, then it means we have not yet reached that level of learning.
Metacognition: Regulating, observing and developing the awareness on the progress of those other dimensions. Allows to create a learning environment that suits best our needs and that’s autonomous. Allows to continue the learning if the circumstances change, such as conditions that are unfavourable to keep the Application stage straight-forward.
On the example of riding a bicycle:
Foundational: Using the muscles, muscle memory and learning the signs.
Application: Getting on the bike and riding it, using the gears, and connecting ourselves to the instrument. Without memory you cannot continue into this stage.
Integration: How the bicycle influences the choices like environmental implications, or for health reasons, or to contemplate nice landscapes from home to work.
Caring: Some aspects of the bicycle might arise, like rain falling while using it. Instead of simply complaining or not being aware of the reason that causes discomfort, it’s important to produce the necessary changes to counteract the bad feelings.
Human: More healthy, awake, aware, sporty, seeing how now he is more environmentally concerned. There’s an effect on it, and people around it see that change, they see him more active.
Metacognition: If one gets old, one can change the path or get an motorized bicycle.
On the example of learning Tibetan:
Foundational: Remembering the letters, the shapes, sounds, combinations, etc.
Application: Building sentences, using the words in the context, etc.
Integration: Using it for prayers, Dharma, to explore the culture, etc.
Caring: Maybe a person likes the letters and sounds, but dislikes the grammar. In that case realising the aversion to grammar comes from previous experiences, then being aware of it and seeing the Tibetan grammar can be different and more interesting might be a good antidote.
Human: If one wants to be a Buddhist and use Tibetan to understand better the concepts through meanings of the composing morphemes or gramatical structures that might carry subtleties that color the idea into a different tone.
Metacognition: The Tibetan language affects the thinking and perception by keeping an environment that’s favourable to keep practicing this learning.
Gardner’s Theory of Multiple Intelligences

All those are present in all beings, in different degrees.
We tend to feel averted to the areas where are less developed. It’s better to open up and not resist to much to change. If you believe you won’t be learning the thing through X intelligence it will never allow experimenting the fruits that intelligence could produce.
Bodily-Kinaesthetic: Moving body, dancing.
Existential: Spiritual intelligent people tend to take alpha role in groups by helping others. That’s one possibility.
Logical-Mathematical: They get patterns easily.
Linguistic: People who have appretiation for languages it might help with the learning process of Tibetan language itself.
Musical: It’s easy for them to understand sound and its creative applications as well as singing or playing instruments.
Visual-Spatial: They easily grasp the script execution.
Interpersonal: Comunicating with languge tutors, and classmates.
Intrapersonal: Comes useful when developing metacognitive skills.
Naturalistic: Anthropology, how it relates to the environment.

ChatGPT Prompt

When you open the app, you see a clean welcome screen. After signing up with email, you land on the main dashboard. Here you see your tasks sorted by AI priority. You can add tasks through quick-add or by chatting with AI. When ready to focus, you enter Focus Mode which blocks notifications and starts a timer. After completing a session, you see your progress and can start another session or take a break.
I am building a productivity app that helps users focus on one task at a time. I need a detailed and structured explanation of the app’s flow and features, written as a Markdown file, so that it is easy for an app developer to understand and implement. Use the Context over to write this.
